text: Hello everyone, so today I will be talking about laparoscopic assisted excision of a pelvic teratoma that we did recently. I was assisted by Dr. Vipul and Dr. Takrooni who is our fellow in paediatric minimal access surgery from Egypt.

So this was an eleven month old girl who presented to us with retention of urine and difficulty in passage of stools and was found to have a very large tumour which was occupying almost the entire pelvis and pressing on the bladder as you can see in the MRI.
The video shows a pelvic teratoma with a predominantly cystic component located in the abdomen and a solid component extending into the perineum and near the sacrum.
The surgeon uses epigastric ports to hold the bladder, with the rectum visible below it. Behind the rectum, a large cystic structure occupies nearly the entire pelvic cavity.

The video shows the cystic component of the pelvic teratoma, with the solid component extending from the S-III and S-IV vertebrae onto the surface in the perineum, located posteriorly between the coccyx and the anal region.
The surgeon begins dissecting the rectum away from the large cystic structure occupying the pelvic cavity, carefully preserving the rectum's vascularity during the procedure.

I think a lot has been said about whether a laparoscopic or a robotic approach is better or is an open approach better. If all of you remember, this has a lot of high incidence of development of neurogenic bladder or neuropraxia to the rectum while removing these tumors and I think that happened mainly because

we would go in blindly, the dissection in the sacrum would not happen under vision and that is what happens with laparoscopic and robotic that you can actually do that dissection under vision and try and preserve those nerves and I think that's the reason why we don't get too much of neurogenic injury in laparoscopic or robot assisted excision.

The surgeon aspirates fluid from the large cystic structure occupying the pelvic cavity, sending it for cytology. The rectum is then hitched to the anterior abdominal wall to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon begins dissecting between the rectum and the cyst, carefully separating the cyst from the mesorectal tissue. The dissection follows the mesorectal plane, which is well-defined and minimizes bleeding.
The surgeon navigates across the mesentery of the rectum, reaching down to the fundus of the cyst. The dissection proceeds by separating the cyst from the rectum.
The surgeon identifies and dissects along a well-defined avascular plane between the rectum and the cyst wall, ensuring preservation of the rectum's vascularity.

Because apart from the nerve injury, I think one big problem that you can face or a big complication that you can face could be the devascularization of the rectum, which in extreme cases can cause complete rectal gangrene and rectal atresia. Or in smaller cases, what it can lead to is rectal stenosis. And then you'll have these patients coming back to you with recalcitrant constipation, which doesn't seem to resolve.
The laparoscopic view provides clear visualization of the anatomical planes, allowing the surgeon to perform atraumatic dissection without damaging surrounding structures.

Now, the area that we are operating in, you have both the ureters entering the pelvic brim by crossing the vessels. You have the falciforms. You have the fallopian tubes, the ovaries right next to it. So there are a lot of structures which can get damaged if your vision is hampered by any chance.

And I don't have anything against open surgeries, but I think this kind of vision in deep pelvic surgery is near about impossible to be achieved with open approach. And I think for these kind of tumors and these kind of masses, a laparoscopic or a robotic approach works brilliantly.
The surgeon has separated the fundus of the cyst, completing the anterior wall dissection. The lateral dissections have been extended up to the level of the ureters.

The surgeon moves posteriorly after freeing the fundus of the cyst, carefully dissecting to avoid damaging the pre-sacral plexus of veins and the parasympathetic outflow, which are critical to prevent excessive bleeding and urogenic bladder complications.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the mass, defining the dissection planes while staying close to the cyst. The final strands of tissue are being separated during the lateral dissection.

And you can use either a monopolar coterie or a bipolar coterie, or you can go for a vessel sealing device, which we will do subsequently once we reach the pre-sacral space. Or you can use a harmonic scalpel. I think choice of dissecting instrument is totally a personal thing, what you prefer to do. and what is available to you in your setup.
The surgeon continues using monopolar cautery to dissect in the pre-sacral space, with no visible bleeding encountered. Additional fluid is aspirated from the cyst to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Covedian Ligature vessel sealing device to separate the tissue from the pre-sacral space and detach it from the sacrum, ensuring minimal current spread to avoid nerve damage while maintaining hemostasis.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the pre-sacral space, staying close to the tumor mass to avoid deeper penetration into the sacrum. Adhesion bands are being removed, with dissection proceeding laterally towards the ischiopubic ramus, ensuring not to extend distally beyond this anatomical landmark.

The surgeon carefully removes residual adhesion bands in the abdominal cavity, ensuring to maintain the correct dissection plane to minimize bleeding risk. The abdominal dissection is completed, and the transition to the perineal approach is planned.

The surgeon loosens the stitch that hitched the cyst to the anterior abdominal wall and dissects the remaining bands, ensuring the cyst wall is completely free from intrapelvic structures in preparation for delivery from the perineum.

The surgeon removes the final adhesions around the cyst, ensuring the dissection is at a consistent level around the S-III and S-IV vertebrae. The peritoneum is carefully dissected away, preparing for the transition to the perineal approach.

The surgeon checks the orientation of the ureter and fallopian tubes, with the uterus visible above the cyst. The rectum is moved to the side, and the surgical field is thoroughly washed to ensure hemostasis. A drain is prepared for placement, either from the perineal part or through the gluteal fold, marking the completion of the abdominal dissection. The rectum and uterus appear undamaged.

The patient is positioned prone, and a central incision is made from the S-V level down to the cyst. The surgeon carefully dissects around the rectum, avoiding damage, and delivers the cyst from the perineum. The coccygectomy is performed to reduce recurrence risk, and the large specimen, approximately twenty centimeters in length, is completely removed.
